infobox.value: was an [armed conflict](/wiki/Armed_conflict "Armed conflict") between the [Mohave people](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") from 1858 to 1859. With the [California Gold Rush](/wiki/California_Gold_Rush "California Gold Rush") of 1849, thousands of American settlers headed west through Mohave country and into [California](/wiki/California "California"). The influx of migrants passing through, combined with simple misunderstandings, led to conflict. [Fort Mohave](/wiki/Fort_Mohave "Fort Mohave") on the [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") side of the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") was built for operations against the Mohave and was the second American military post established on the river after [Fort Yuma](/wiki/Fort_Yuma "Fort Yuma"). Eventually advantages in weaponry and tactics brought the Mohave and their allies to surrender. After the signing of a peace treaty in 1859, the Mohave no longer opposed the United States through warfare. The peace also ended a long [guerrilla war](/wiki/Guerrilla_war "Guerrilla war") between the Mohave and the [Maricopa](/wiki/Maricopa_people "Maricopa people") of south central Arizona.[[1]](#cite_note-mohavedesert.net-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Kroeber-2)

The **Mohave War** was an [armed conflict](/wiki/Armed_conflict "Armed conflict") between the [Mohave people](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") from 1858 to 1859. With the [California Gold Rush](/wiki/California_Gold_Rush "California Gold Rush") of 1849, thousands of American settlers headed west through Mohave country and into [California](/wiki/California "California"). The influx of migrants passing through, combined with simple misunderstandings, led to conflict. [Fort Mohave](/wiki/Fort_Mohave "Fort Mohave") on the [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") side of the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") was built for operations against the Mohave and was the second American military post established on the river after [Fort Yuma](/wiki/Fort_Yuma "Fort Yuma"). Eventually advantages in weaponry and tactics brought the Mohave and their allies to surrender. After the signing of a peace treaty in 1859, the Mohave no longer opposed the United States through warfare. The peace also ended a long [guerrilla war](/wiki/Guerrilla_war "Guerrilla war") between the Mohave and the [Maricopa](/wiki/Maricopa_people "Maricopa people") of south central Arizona.[[1]](#cite_note-mohavedesert.net-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Kroeber-2)

## Battle of Beale's Crossing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohave_War&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Battle of Beale's Crossing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Fort_Mohave_sketch.gif/250px-Fort_Mohave_sketch.gif)](/wiki/File:Fort_Mohave_sketch.gif)

A sketch of Fort Mohave in the late 1800s

In 1857, [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [James Buchanan](/wiki/James_Buchanan "James Buchanan") appointed [Edward Fitzgerald Beale](/wiki/Edward_Fitzgerald_Beale "Edward Fitzgerald Beale") to survey and build a 1,000 mile (1,600 km) road from [Fort Defiance](/wiki/Fort_Defiance,_Arizona "Fort Defiance, Arizona") in northern [New Mexico Territory](/wiki/New_Mexico_Territory "New Mexico Territory"), to the Colorado River. The road was meant for immigrants, traveling to the California gold fields and crossed the Colorado at [Beale's Crossing](/wiki/Beale%27s_Crossing "Beale's Crossing") within Mohave territory. Beale's Crossing in 1857 was little more than a shallow point in the river until a party of emigrants known as the [Rose-Baley Party](/wiki/Rose-Baley_Party "Rose-Baley Party") arrived. The wagon train set out from [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico") and when they got to the Colorado, they made camp and built a dock for the construction of [rafts](/wiki/Raft "Raft") to ferry the heavier wagon equipment across. [Chiefs](/wiki/Tribal_chief "Tribal chief") [Irataba](/wiki/Irataba "Irataba"), [Cairook](/wiki/Cairook "Cairook") and [Sickahot](/wiki/Sickahot "Sickahot") approached the American settlers and in a [pow wow](/wiki/Pow_wow "Pow wow") the Mohave asked the party's leader, L. J. Rose if they intended to build a town along the river. Rose responded with no and informed the chiefs that they were headed for California and would only be staying for a short while.[[1]](#cite_note-mohavedesert.net-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Kroeber,_pg._11-12-3)

Tension arose when the Mohave learned that the Bailey company would be coming through just after the Rose company, so they began stealing cattle for the meat. The Americans noticed that some of their herd had gone missing and when they caught the Mohaves in the act L. R. Rose confronted them, but the Mohave responded as though it was a joke. Also, near the American camp was a patch of [cottonwood](/wiki/Populus_sect._Aegiros "Populus sect. Aegiros") trees that belonged to the Mohave who used it for buildings and clothing. Rose and his men did not realize that the trees were Mohave property so they were cut down and used to build the rafts. When the Mohave discovered that their valuable cottonwoods had been disturbed, they responded by attacking the settlers with their [Walapai](/wiki/Walapai "Walapai") allies. The Rose Party included dozens of women and children with only twenty-five men at arms fighting against hundreds of Mohave [warriors](/wiki/Warrior "Warrior"). The Walapai with a small bunch of Mohaves focused on attacking four women and children held up in a cabin built in the mountains outside of the camp while the main Mohave force attacked the camp itself. In the end seventeen warriors were killed and left on the battlefield while the Americans lost one killed, Alpha Brown, and eleven seriously injured. The settlers won the day by repulsing the Mohave attack but they lost most of their livestock and instead of crossing the border into California, they turned around and went back to New Mexico.[[1]](#cite_note-mohavedesert.net-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Kroeber,_pg._11-12-3)

Irataba and his band of Mohaves, who had been key in establishing peace at the end of the [Yuma War](/wiki/Yuma_War "Yuma War"), were against conflict with the United States and later Irataba became a high-ranking leader among his people after helping end the Mohave War. The war with the Maricopa was also ongoing and this was a major reason why Irataba was for cooperating with the Americans. Irataba's opinion was not accepted in 1858 though, other important chiefs were for war with the United States, including Cairook and Sickahot. The other chiefs were highly popular among their warriors so Irataba was powerless to stop them.[[1]](#cite_note-mohavedesert.net-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Kroeber,_pg._11-12-3)

## First Mohave Expedition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohave_War&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: First Mohave Expedition")]

News of the battle at Beale's Crossing reached the media quickly followed by the [United States War Department](/wiki/United_States_War_Department "United States War Department") and [General](/wiki/General "General") [Newman S. Clarke](/wiki/Newman_S._Clarke "Newman S. Clarke") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). Clarke sent [Lieutenant Colonel](/wiki/Lieutenant_Colonel "Lieutenant Colonel") [William Hoffman](/wiki/William_Hoffman_(U.S._Army) "William Hoffman (U.S. Army)") of the [6th Cavalry](/wiki/6th_Cavalry_Regiment_(United_States) "6th Cavalry Regiment (United States)") on an expedition to Beale's Crossing to build a post for protecting settlers crossing the Colorado. Hoffman set out from a ranch in [Cajon Canyon](/wiki/Cajon_Canyon "Cajon Canyon") on December 28, 1858, hoping to make it across the [Mohave Desert](/wiki/Mohave_Desert "Mohave Desert") along the [Mohave Road](/wiki/Mojave_Road "Mojave Road") before the heat of summer could set in.[[1]](#cite_note-mohavedesert.net-1)

In January 1859, Hoffman was encamped with his fifty men near [Beaver Lake](/wiki/Beaver_Lake_(Nevada) "Beaver Lake (Nevada)"). Chief Cairook was in the area with about 300 men and the two sides encountered one another in an [Indian war](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars") [skirmish](/wiki/Skirmish "Skirmish") known as the [Battle of Beaver Lake](/w/index.php?title=Battle_of_Beaver_Lake&action=edit&redlink=1 "Battle of Beaver Lake (page does not exist)"). The American camp was surrounded but accurate musket fire proved deadly for the Mohaves and ten to twelve were seen to fall according to Hoffman's account. The Americans went back to base after this fight without establishing a post on the Colorado. On March 1, Lieutenant [Samuel A. Bishop](/wiki/Samuel_A._Bishop "Samuel A. Bishop") was sent from Los Angeles to deliver fresh supplies to Lieutenant Edward Beale who was working on his road in northern Arizona. With thirty-eight men on [camels](/wiki/Camel "Camel") and several wagons and mules, the column was met at [Cave Canyon](/w/index.php?title=Cave_Canyon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cave Canyon (page does not exist)") in the Mohave Desert by employees of the [Central Overland Mail](/wiki/Central_Overland_Route "Central Overland Route") company and soon after by 1,500 warriors. The Mohaves opened fire on the small troop of Americans but they reportedly missed their targets intentionally but later moved in closer for a deliberate attack. In the ensuing skirmish, two natives were wounded by musketry, one of whom died later on. Lieutenant Bishop decided it would be best to send the wagons back to the coast while he successfully proceeded to Beale's camp.[[1]](#cite_note-mohavedesert.net-1)

## Second Mohave Expedition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohave_War&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Second Mohave Expedition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Two_Mohave_braves%2C_western_Arizona_-_Timothy_O%27Sullivan_-_NARA.jpg/250px-Two_Mohave_braves%2C_western_Arizona_-_Timothy_O%27Sullivan_-_NARA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Two_Mohave_braves,_western_Arizona_-_Timothy_O%27Sullivan_-_NARA.jpg)

Two Mohave men next to the Colorado River in 1871

Lieutenant Colonel Hoffman used the battle at [Beaver Lake](/wiki/Beaver_Lake_(Nevada) "Beaver Lake (Nevada)") as justification to ask the War Department for more troops. So in April 1859, another expedition was sent to the Colorado which included over 600 [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"), [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") and [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery"). After establishing [Camp Gaston](/wiki/Camp_Gaston "Camp Gaston") as an advance base, Hoffman's second expedition reached Beale's Crossing and [Camp Colorado](/wiki/Fort_Mohave "Fort Mohave") was officially established on April 19 and nine days later it was renamed [Fort Mohave](/wiki/Fort_Mohave "Fort Mohave") by [Captain](/wiki/Captain_(land) "Captain (land)") [Lewis Addison Armistead](/wiki/Lewis_Addison_Armistead "Lewis Addison Armistead"). On April 23, Lieutenant Colonel Hoffman arrived at the Mohave villages and discussed peace with the inhabitants. Hoffman told the chiefs that if they would agree to not attack immigrants traveling through their land and not oppose the government's building of roads and forts in Mohave territory, then they would not be destroyed. Reluctantly the Mohave agreed and Chief Cairook surrendered himself and was taken down the Colorado to Fort Yuma with eight others. Hoffman then went to [San Bernardino](/wiki/San_Bernardino "San Bernardino") and left Captain Armistead of the [6th Infantry](/wiki/6th_Infantry_Regiment_(United_States) "6th Infantry Regiment (United States)") in charge of Fort Mohave with fifty men and a few artillery pieces. Meanwhile, troops and two [river steamers](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") were waiting at Fort Yuma to reinforce Fort Mohave if necessary.[[4]](#cite_note-mohavedesert.net1-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Lingenfelter-5): 23–24

## Escape from Fort Yuma

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohave_War&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Escape from Fort Yuma")]

After several weeks of a long hot summer in a cell, Chief Cairook and the eight others plotted their escape. Every day the prisoners were allowed to leave the cell for fresh air; and it was during one of these recesses in late June that the simple plan unfolded. Cairook seized one of his sentries while the eight others ran for the brush. The other sentries noticed and opened fire on the fleeing Mohaves; one was killed and Cairook was bayoneted and shot. Three of the Mohaves eventually made it back to their villages and told the high chief, [Espaniole](/w/index.php?title=Espaniole&action=edit&redlink=1 "Espaniole (page does not exist)"), who decided to end the peace and attack a mail station two miles south of Fort Mohave. There the station was destroyed along with a field of melons planted by the Americans. When Captain Armistead learned of this he launched a series of small operations against the Mohaves with his fifty men and engaged in several small skirmishes.[[4]](#cite_note-mohavedesert.net1-4)

## Battle of the Colorado River

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohave_War&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Battle of the Colorado River")]

It was not until August 4, 1859 that the only pitched battle of the war was fought. Captain Armstead and [First Lieutenant](/wiki/First_Lieutenant "First Lieutenant") [Elisha Marshall](/wiki/Elisha_Marshall "Elisha Marshall") led fifty men in an attack on over 200 Mohaves twelve miles south of the fort along the Colorado River. The natives were routed, leaving twenty-three warriors on the field, while only three Americans were wounded. Next the Mohave under the direction of Chief Espaniole sued for peace and the war was over. There was still a group among the Mohaves that wanted to continue fighting the United States, though no renewed conflict came about. Irataba, with support of the United States Army, became a prominent leader and was considered vital for maintaining the peace. When word of peace reached the Maricopa, they sent Chief [Ahwantsevarih](/w/index.php?title=Ahwantsevarih&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ahwantsevarih (page does not exist)") to Mohave territory to end their war, which was little more than minor raiding, with the exception of one [engagement at Pima Butte](/wiki/Battle_of_Pima_Butte "Battle of Pima Butte") in 1857. Irataba met with Ahwantsevarih, and the two chiefs agreed to have peace and establish trade relations.[[4]](#cite_note-mohavedesert.net1-4)[[6]](#cite_note-6)
